text: The robotic surgical system is positioned at a forty-five degree angle from the left side of the patient. Three robotic ports are utilized, with the central camera port located one inch above and lateral to the umbilicus.
The right robotic assist port is positioned eight centimeters to the right of the central camera port, and the left robotic assist port is positioned eight centimeters to the left of the camera port.
Two extra assist ports are utilized, with the right iliac fossa featuring a twelve millimeter assist port for introducing swabs or Johan's retractors to retract or move the bowel.
The surgeon utilizes the air sail equipment through the left assist port to maintain pneumoperitoneum and remove gas during the procedure.

The surgeon utilizes a 30-degree down camera through the central camera port, while a curved monopolar scissors is inserted through the right assist port and a bipolar grasper through the left robotic assist port.
The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) after completing the medial to lateral dissection, focusing on the rectal area.

The surgeon lifts the rectum using a bipolar grasp in the left hand, stretching the tissue to reveal the delicate angel hair fibers that define the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The bipolar grasp is also used to control any bleeding that occurs during this maneuver.

The surgeon uses the left robotic bipolar grasper to stretch the rectum, clearly displaying the angiolab fibers. The hypogastric nerves come into view, highlighting the importance of preserving these structures during the dissection.

The surgeon stretches the rectum using robotic instruments, revealing the angiolab fibers that guide the dissection path. The angel hair fibers help accurately identify the TME plane, ensuring preservation of key structures and hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon continues the dissection down to the pelvic floor, using the left articulated wrist to maintain traction and put the rectum on stretch.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction with robotic technology to demonstrate the angiolab fibers, guiding the dissection in the deep pelvis. The rectum curves as the dissection follows the mesorectum, avoiding pelvic vessels to prevent complications and bleeding.

The surgeon maintains stretch on the rectum using a robotic instrument, clearly demonstrating the angiolyte fibers. The articulated instrument tips are used to navigate challenging angles, revealing the pelvic floor as the dissection progresses posteriorly.

The surgeon frees the rectum from the right pelvic sidewall and proceeds with anterior dissection. The peritoneum is incised, revealing the Denonvilliers fascia, while ensuring the surrounding fat remains attached to the rectum.
The surgeon identifies a clear fascial plane that differentiates the denonvilliers fascia from the fat on the rectum, ensuring to stay within this plane during dissection.

The surgeon uses traction on the rectum with the left instrument to identify a clear plane of dissection. The dissection is carried anterolaterally, then anteriorly, and finally to the left lateral part of the rectum, ensuring the denonvillous fascia is preserved to protect the nerves.
The surgeon frees the distal sigmoid and upper rectum from the left pelvic side wall by opening the peritoneal attachments. This allows access to the previously dissected medial to lateral plane.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum on the left side, revealing the ureter and gonadal vessels in the surgical field.

The surgeon frees the proximal sigmoid attachments through careful dissection. An ethyl on stitch is used with a straight needle to elevate the anterior pelvic peritoneal folds, keeping them out of the way during the anterior dissection.
The surgeon pulls the rectum to the right while unzipping the left peritoneal attachments, freeing the rectum completely from the lateral pelvic sidewalls.

The surgeon uses the tip of monopolar scissors like a paintbrush to divide the pelvic attachments, freeing the rectum from the left pelvic sidewall. The dissection progresses from the left lateral sidewall to the anterior aspect.

The surgeon applies traction to the rectum using the left instrument, stretching the tissues and revealing the angiolyte fibers. The dissection is focused on maintaining the correct plane in the low anterior region.

The surgeon demonstrates traction and counter-traction techniques during low pelvic dissection, stretching the tissues to expose angiolab fibers. Careful dissection is performed to avoid damaging the rectum and surrounding structures in the low pelvis.
The surgeon approaches the intersphincteric space and focuses on the rectal tube. In the deep posterior pelvis, the remaining fibers holding the rectum are dissected, freeing the rectum completely.
The surgeon performs a rectal examination to assess the level of the rectum, preparing for the final step of cross-stapling the rectum on the pelvic floor.
The surgeon uses a Covedian tri-staple purple cartridge stapler with an external articulating lever to perform a very low staple on the pelvic floor.

The surgeon positions the thicker jaw of the stapler posteriorly to the rectum to avoid damaging the rectal wall. The stapler is manipulated using the lever to align parallel to the pelvic floor, and two staple fires are executed to cross the rectum at a very low level.
